content.text: are two fundamental concepts in software testing that help create reliable, isolated, and maintainable test environments. They are both types of
(fake replacements for real dependencies), but serve distinct purposes:
: Predefined sample data or state that answers
: Simulated object that verifies interactions and answers
provides a "defined, reliable and consistent context for tests" — including environment (database with known parameters) or content (dataset). A
is a "simulated object that mimics behavior of real objects" and can be configured to return specific values or verify method calls.

Without fixtures and mocks:
- Tests fail due to external dependencies (databases, APIs) being unavailable
- Test data is inconsistent, making results unreliable
- Tests are slow (real database calls, network requests)
- Tests interfere with each other through shared state
- You can't verify how code interacts with dependencies
tests that truly protect your code.
✅ Create data once, use across tests
❌ Each test needs its own mock setup
⚠️ Need updates when data model changes
⚠️ High (mocks may not match real behavior)
Fixture: What Data Do I Start With?
for your models — a convenient way to create realistic objects without filling in every field manually.
Test → Fixture (data) → Your Code → Expected Output
Mock: Was This Dependency Called Correctly?
so you can assert on them. You verify not just what code returned, but
Test → Mock (fake dependency) → Your Code → Verify Interaction
Key Distinction: Fixture vs Stub vs Mock
What data do I start with?
Build test data with sensible defaults

: "Give me data" — returns fixed data, doesn't verify calls
-
: "Give me data AND check how I'm being used" — verifies behavior

: Don't use for dynamic data that changes per test
-
: Don't mock your own code (only external dependencies), don't over-mock (can hide real bugs)

: Mark function as fixture
- Fixtures can be

from unittest.mock import Mock

# Mock: simulated email service
def test_send_welcome_email():
    # Create mock email service
    mock_email_service = Mock()
    mock_email_service.send.return_value = True
    
    # Use mock in user service
    user_service = UserService(mock_email_service)
    user_service.send_welcome_email(user_id=1)
    
    # Verify interaction (NOT just return value)
    mock_email_service.send.assert_called_once_with(
        user_id=1,
        template="welcome"
    )
Swift Testing Example (Fixture + Stub + Mock Together)
Use stubs for simple return values
Don't over-mock when just need data
Complete Example: Testing Email Notification Service

from unittest.mock import Mock
import pytest

# Fixture: test user data
@pytest.fixture
def new_user():
    return {
        "id": 123,
        "name": "John Doe",
        "email": "john@example.com"
    }

# Mock: fake email service
def test_send_notification_sends_email(new_user):
    # Create mock email service
    mock_email_service = Mock()
    mock_email_service.send.return_value = True
    
    # Create notification service with mock
    notification = NotificationService(mock_email_service)
    
    # Execute
    notification.send_welcome(new_user)
    
    # Verify fixture data was used
    assert mock_email_service.send.called
    
    # Verify mock interaction (behavior verification)
    mock_email_service.send.assert_called_once_with(
        to="john@example.com",
        template="welcome",
        subject="Welcome John Doe!"
    )
: Assert method was called with specific arguments

: 200+ fixtures for: - User data (admin, regular, guest) - Product data (different categories, prices) - Configuration (environment settings)
-

: 150+ mocks for: - API clients (external services) - Database connections (unit tests) - Email/SMS services - Analytics trackers
-

: 80% of tests use both fixtures + mocks
-
: Tests run in 30 seconds (vs 10 minutes without), 95%+ reliability
Stubs are simpler for just data
Use stubs when you only need return values
Assert method calls when using mocks

— fixtures for data, stubs for return values, mocks for verification
— clean state before and after each test
— mocks excel at checking method calls
— too many mocks hide real integration bugs
